hard.next_step: 6/29 - Great discovery. Demo re-scheduled for 7/8 6/9 - Kristopher scheduled discovery for 6/15 5/26 - Engagement in Showpad but no correspondence from Kristopher yet. 5/13 - Detailed intro call. Krisopher (VP of FInance) will review presentation and follow up with available times for a disocvery call. Tentatively week of 5/18 Steve Zycus AE Send recap email with key pain points and Zycus fit. Share the platform deck/materials discussed. Coordinate a 30-minute discovery call. Bring in a Solutions Consultant for deeper scoping. Align next call around their main priority Spend Analysis, CLM, and Sourcing. Kristopher VP Finance Review Zycus materials after the call. Validate internal priority areas: spend, contracts, sourcing, invoicing. Prepare follow-up questions on ROI and implementation. Confirm availability for discovery call. Share current process details around invoices, contracts, and sourcing. Prashant BDR Help coordinate a Discovery and Demo call.
hard.ais_why:
• Top-down AI mandate confirmed on the May 7 call — the CEO is driving AI initiatives across functions including procurement, with the customer exploring AI for PDF data extraction and anomaly detection in negotiations.
• Customer voiced interest in autonomous sourcing and AI-driven supplier discovery; rep walked through Teams intake, Spendminer, autonomous negotiation, CLM and supplier risk in response.
• SF description echoes the CEO AI push and anomaly detection ask, with next steps prioritizing Spend Analysis, CLM and Sourcing. Very recent activity (5/12).

north_star_verdict.headline: Live, well-qualified Formal Evaluation with an engaged VP-Finance champion who proactively rescheduled the platform demo to 8 Jul. 85 days to close is comfortable. The deal is won or lost on one thing: proving ROI on low purchase volume to kill the 'you're too small' narrative three rival vendors planted; the economic-buyer gap is real but downstream of that.
north_star_verdict.math: Created 7 May, ~60 days in cycle. In Formal Evaluation since 16 Jun, 85 days to close (29 Sep, pushed from 1 Aug on 29 Jun). Buyer proactively offered two demo slots on 25 Jun and the demo is set for 8 Jul: genuine two-way engagement, not rep activity. Path from demo (8 Jul) to low-volume ROI case + healthcare-MSO references to champion-brokered EB intro to Stampley/NV integration scoping to contracting fits inside a 29 Sep close. Date is credible; the risk is qualitative (unproven ROI, unmapped EB, do-nothing), not timing.
evidence: Kristopher Nelson proactively rescheduled the demo on 25 Jun citing quarter-end and offered two new slots for the week of 7/6 (Clari email received 25 Jun); demo now set for 8 Jul: active buyer engagement, not a stall.

Best Case is not yet defensible: no quantified ROI/business case built, no economic buyer engaged, exploratory buyer posture ('it depends on the ROI', 15 Jun). Honest category is Pipeline until the ROI case lands and the EB is mapped.

metrics.narrative: Pain is clear but never quantified. Kristopher Nelson framed the goal as ROI on a fully manual Capex process ('it takes us a couple hours to procure a piece of equipment instead of a couple weeks', 7 May) and painful invoice price lookups (digging through PDFs in Stampley to find one item's price, 7 May). No dollar baseline, spend-under-management figure, or savings target was stated: 'I'd have to see what kind of savings we could potentially get to really balance that off of the initial cost' (15 Jun). Quantifying this via the ROI business case Steve offered is the single highest-leverage move, and it doubles as the proof that defeats the vendor-fit objection.

economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer is engaged. Kristopher Nelson (VP Finance, Decision Maker) deferred budget upward: 'I don't really have a targeted budget... our leadership's open to new AI tools... whatever the price tag ends up being is a huge factor' (15 Jun). The CEO is the named AI sponsor who tasked Kristopher ('our CEO is gung ho about AI now... he tasked me with procurement supply chain', 7 May) but has never engaged. Salesforce names no economic-buyer contact, so the CEO/CFO stays a role, not a verified name. The correct move is to have Kristopher broker the EB intro ONCE the ROI case is in hand; an EB intro before there is a quantified business case has nothing to land on.

decision_criteria.narrative: The buyer's stated decision criteria are ROI/savings first ('we're looking for what kind of value can we add... is there individual pricing savings', 7 May), a single unified platform ('I like that it's one platform', 7 May), and minimal manual lift ('the minimal legwork that we have to do, the better', 15 Jun). Kristopher also values a phased, low-risk start scoped to Capex sourcing rather than a full-suite rollout. Criteria are buyer-confirmed but not yet formalized into an RFP or scorecard; there has been no ShoeFit/BRD round, so weighting and must-haves are still inferred, not documented.

decision_process.narrative: Read from the call sequence: a 7 May intro (Prashant Saxena BDR + Steve Ovadje AE with Kristopher), then a 15 Jun 30-minute discovery run by the relevant stakeholder with Kristopher and Paul Hernandez, and an hour-plus module demo set for 8 Jul. Kristopher controls the evaluation within his remit but flagged that anything touching accounting/invoicing 'is in a whole other world... it's not my realm' (15 Jun), signaling approvals and integration decisions escalate beyond him. NVISION's own internal approval chain for the spend it wants to automate runs operations leadership then the chief medical officer for any equipment over $10k (15 Jun). The vendor-selection approval chain above Kristopher (CFO/CEO sign-off on a ~$130k buy) is not yet mapped.
sources: Avoma intro call 07 May 2026 (Kristopher Nelson, Steve Ovadje, Prashant Saxena)
Avoma discovery call 15 Jun 2026 (Kristopher Nelson, Paul Hernandez, Ritika)

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics are unexplored: no MSA, SOW, legal, procurement-channel or InfoSec discussion yet, which is normal for an early Formal Evaluation. Two integration dependencies will likely trigger IT/InfoSec review before paper: eProcurement/eInvoicing coexisting with or replacing Stampley (the AP tool where all invoices sit today) and the NV medical-supply tool, both of which Kristopher flagged as outside his control ('Stamply, that's not my world... it would be a whole other lift to get that team involved', 15 Jun). This is a single-module-plus-suite new-business deal, so expect an Order Form + SOW path; scope the phase-1 integration footprint at the demo and confirm who owns IT/security sign-off.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is specific, multi-point and buyer-confirmed. Capex sourcing is fully manual ('in your world it's kicking off an RFQ; in our world it's really emails go out, phone calls go out... it's all outside of the system... it's kind of a mess to go back and audit', 15 Jun). Contracts are an unstructured PDF 'dumping ground' saved to a shared site with no repository (7 May). Invoices sit in Stampley, not the ERP, so a single price lookup means digging through PDFs (7 May). There are no compliance controls on off-platform field purchases at the ~100 centers ('we see it when we pay the invoice... no real controls in place', 15 Jun). The pain owner is Kristopher Nelson; day-to-day is Paul Hernandez. The open question is whether the dollar volume justifies the spend (the do-nothing risk), not whether pain exists.

champion.narrative: Kristopher Nelson (VP Finance) is a developing champion: he carries the AI mandate, drives cadence (scheduled the discovery, proactively rescheduled the demo, engaged the Showpad shared space), articulated detailed pain and a clear 'start small, prove ROI, then scale' vision. He is not yet a sponsoring champion: he has no budget authority ('I don't really have a targeted budget'), has not brokered the CEO/CFO intro, and frames the project as one of several priorities ('I have been pulled in a number of areas and this project has not been the priority', 1 Jun). He brought Paul Hernandez (day-to-day procurement) into discovery, but IT and the economic buyer are still absent. Convert him by arming him with an ROI case strong enough to take upstairs.

competition.narrative: The dominant competitor is do-nothing/inertia fueled by vendor-fit skepticism. Kristopher reported multiple vendors dismissed NVISION: 'JAGGAER basically off the top said you guys aren't a good fit... based on your data set we can't help you... your volume is so low... you're not gonna get the ROI from us' (7 May). Coupa, GEP and Zip were earlier touchpoints he named ('I've been talking with Coupa, GEP, Zip', 7 May) but none are in an active bake-off. Zycus's wedge is that Steve countered the fit objection head-on ('we work with SMB, mid-market and large enterprises... we go toe to toe with them', 7 May) and positioned single-code-base AI across all modules (Merlin Intake in Teams, Spendminer, ANA). Win path: prove ROI on low volume using PDF invoice/contract data (no data warehouse needed) plus comparable healthcare-MSO references. Lose, and it dies to do-nothing.
sources: Avoma intro call 07 May 2026 (Kristopher Nelson, Steve Ovadje)

deal_movement.summary: The deal is live and progressing on the buyer's pull. Since the last sweep, stage advanced to Formal Evaluation (16 Jun), the manager set forecast to Best Case (17 Jun), and the close date was pushed to 29 Sep (29 Jun). The last genuine two-way touch was Kristopher's 25 Jun email rescheduling the demo to the week of 7/6; the demo is now set for 8 Jul.

items.change: Buyer proactively rescheduled the platform demo to the week of 7/6 and offered two slots; demo set for 8 Jul
Stage advanced Qualified to Formal Evaluation (Steve Ovadje)
Forecast category moved Pipeline to Best Case (Michael McCarthy)
Close date pushed 1 Aug to 29 Sep (Steve Ovadje)
Discovery call scoped iSource, Merlin Intake, eProcurement and ANA for Capex sourcing

competitive_position.summary: The single strongest CURRENT threat is do-nothing/inertia fueled by vendor-fit skepticism: multiple vendors told NVISION its low volume makes ROI unlikely (Kristopher, 7 May). Coupa, GEP and Zip were named as earlier conversations but are not in an active bake-off; JAGGAER self-disqualified. No competitor is running a parallel evaluation, so the deal is Zycus's to lose to inertia.

competitors.name: Do-nothing / status quo (manual process)
competitors.quote: based on your data set, we can't help you... your volume is so low... you're not gonna get the ROI from us
competitors.how_we_win: Build a concrete low-volume ROI case on PDF invoice/contract data (no warehouse needed) and supply 2-3 comparable healthcare-MSO references to kill the 'too small to benefit' narrative.

competitors.name: Coupa
competitors.quote: I've been talking with Coupa, GEP, Zip
competitors.how_we_win: Single-code-base platform with embedded AI across all modules vs assembled point solutions; Steve noted Zycus sits above Coupa on the 2025 Gartner Magic Quadrant.

competitors.name: GEP
competitors.quote: I've been talking with Coupa, GEP, Zip
competitors.how_we_win: Lead with Merlin Intake in Teams + ANA for low-touch tail-spend automation fit to a lean finance team.

competitors.name: Zip
competitors.quote: I've been talking with Coupa, GEP, Zip
competitors.how_we_win: Full source-to-pay depth vs intake-orchestration-only scope.

competitors.name: JAGGAER
competitors.quote: Jagger basically off the top said, oh, you guys aren't a good fit for us
competitors.how_we_win: Already self-eliminated; use it as proof Zycus serves the segment others won't.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Kristopher's expectations center on provable ROI on low volume, a single unified AI platform, and minimal manual lift. Zycus is aligned on the platform and automation story but exposed on the one criterion that decides the deal: quantified savings on a low-volume spend base.

items.criterion: Minimal manual lift / high automation for a lean team

items.criterion: Integration with existing tools (Stampley for invoices, NV for medical supplies)
items.quote: Stamply, that's not my world... it would be a whole other lift to try to get that team to then get involved

items.requirement: Show quantified low-volume ROI/savings before any budget commitment.
items.quote: I'd have to see what kind of savings we could potentially get to really balance that off of the initial cost

items.requirement: Focus the 8 Jul demo on sourcing, spend analysis and contract management for Capex.

I don't really have, like, a targeted budget or anything

best_practice_check.summary: Three win-strategy levers matter most now: prove low-volume ROI to beat do-nothing, map the economic buyer above Kristopher, and multi-thread into procurement and IT.
flags: Prove low-volume ROI on PDF invoice/contract data to kill the 'too small to benefit' narrative three rival vendors planted: this is the single win lever for THIS deal.
Map and engage the economic buyer (CEO/CFO) via Kristopher once the ROI case lands; budget sits above him on a ~$130k buy and no EB has engaged.
Multi-thread beyond Kristopher into Paul Hernandez (day-to-day procurement) and an IT/InfoSec owner for the Stampley and NV integrations to de-risk adoption.

items.name: Kristopher Nelson
items.title: VP of Finance
items.risk: Developing champion with no budget authority who treats the project as one of several priorities; elevate by arming him with an ROI case he can take to the CEO/CFO.

items.name: Paul Hernandez
items.title: Procurement, Insurance Manager, Real Estate
items.risk: Attended discovery but stayed silent; the day-to-day procurement owner whose buy-in drives adoption. Activate him at the 8 Jul demo.

champion_strength.summary: Kristopher Nelson is a developing champion: engaged, drives cadence, carries the CEO AI mandate and has a clear phase-1 vision, but lacks budget authority and has not yet brokered the EB intro or brought IT into the room. What has developed: he proactively drives the demo cadence and engaged the Showpad content. What is missing: budget authority, an EB introduction, and IT/procurement co-sponsorship.
champion_strength.source: Avoma 07 May & 15 Jun calls; Clari emails 01-25 Jun 2026

ai_positioning_strength.summary: Zycus led the AI story hard and it landed: Steve and Ritika walked Merlin Intake in Teams, Spendminer, ANA autonomous negotiation and AI RFP scoring across both calls, directly matched to the CEO's AI mandate and Kristopher's PDF-data/anomaly-detection ask. The narrative is well positioned; the gap is proof, not story.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AIS 9.0 / Strong is corroborated by the calls: a top-down CEO AI mandate ('our CEO is gung ho about AI now', 7 May), explicit interest in AI for PDF data extraction, anomaly detection and autonomous sourcing, and receptive reactions to Merlin Intake and ANA. The field is accurate, not over-rated.

items.detail: No economic buyer engaged; budget authority sits above Kristopher and no dollar target exists yet.
Do-nothing/inertia is the dominant threat, reinforced by rival vendors' 'too small for ROI' dismissals; buyer wants to start small and phased.
eInvoicing/eProcurement depend on Stampley and NV integrations that sit outside Kristopher's control and will pull in an unmapped IT/AP team.
Unproven ROI on low volume; comparable healthcare-MSO references are the fastest way to break the vendor-fit skepticism.

confidence_signals.summary: The deal is live and buyer-pulled: Kristopher proactively rescheduled and offered demo slots on 25 Jun and the demo is set for 8 Jul. Engagement is single-threaded and commitment is ROI-gated, but there is no cooling.
items: Buyer offered two demo slots and rescheduled proactively (25 Jun): pull, not push.
Engagement is single-threaded on Kristopher; Paul silent, IT and EB absent.

deal_scores_evidence.summary: Live, well-qualified Formal Evaluation with an engaged VP-Finance champion racing to an 8 Jul platform demo; the deal is won or lost on proving ROI on low purchase volume to kill the 'you're too small' narrative three rival vendors already planted, with no economic buyer yet engaged above Kristopher.

win_position.text: Why this number: Formal Evaluation caps confidence in the mid-range; the deal earns it on a Strong AIS (9/Strong CEO AI mandate), detailed buyer-confirmed pain and a developing VP-Finance champion, but is held down by an unproven ROI and no engaged economic buyer.
Do-nothing is the dominant threat: multiple vendors told NVISION its low volume makes ROI unlikely ('your volume is so low... you're not gonna get the ROI', Kristopher, 7 May).
No economic buyer has engaged; budget sits above Kristopher ('I don't really have a targeted budget', 15 Jun), so a champion win still has to clear an unmapped CEO/CFO gate.
Kristopher praised the single-platform model ('I like that it's one platform', 7 May), drives the cadence, and carries the CEO's AI mandate: a real champion, if a developing one.

deal_momentum.text: Buyer proactively rescheduled and offered two demo slots for the week of 7/6, with the demo now set for 8 Jul (Clari email 25 Jun): pull, not push.
Structured 15 Jun discovery scoped iSource, Merlin Intake, eProcurement and ANA for Capex, advancing beyond a generic demo.
Stage moved Qualified to Formal Evaluation (16 Jun) but the close date was pushed 1 Aug to 29 Sep (29 Jun): the timeline is elongating, not tightening.

customer_commitment.text: Posture is exploratory and ROI-gated: 'really, I don't have a specific ask or need, it's really what is out there' (7 May).
Kristopher engaged the Showpad shared space and brought Paul Hernandez, the day-to-day procurement owner, into discovery.
He frames the project as one of several competing priorities: 'I have been pulled in a number of areas and this project has not been the priority' (1 Jun).

deal_risk.text: Do-nothing/inertia is the top threat, reinforced by rival vendors' 'too small for ROI' dismissals (7 May).
No economic buyer is mapped or engaged; budget authority sits above Kristopher.
Integration dependencies (Stampley, NV) sit outside Kristopher's control ('that's not my world... a whole other lift', 15 Jun) and will pull in an unmapped IT/AP team.
Scope is narrowing to a phased Capex-only start, a cost-cautious defensive signal.

customer_commitment.evidence: Exploratory, ROI-gated posture and project framed as one of several priorities (7 May, 1 Jun).

deal_risk.evidence: Do-nothing dominant, no EB engaged, integration outside champion's control, scope narrowing (7 May-15 Jun).

scope_change.from: Full source-to-pay suite (iContract, iSource, iSave, eProcurement, eInvoicing, Merlin, Merlin Intake, ANA) across the company
scope_change.to: A phased Capex-focused start (sourcing, spend analysis, contract management) for large-ticket equipment only
scope_change.detail: Kristopher wants to start small: 'I don't wanna, like, just jump all in and get all the areas all at once, which is why I'm trying to narrow it down' (15 Jun); he flagged invoicing/eProcurement as longer-term and 'a bigger change'. A defensive, cost-cautious phased posture, not a lost deal: land phase 1, then expand.

items.action: Deliver the 8 Jul demo on iSource, Merlin Intake, eProcurement and ANA for Capex.
items.trigger: Buyer rescheduled and set the demo for 8 Jul focused on their large-ticket sourcing priority
items.expected_effect: Converts discovery into a scoped phase-1 vision and sets up the ROI case; keeps the 29 Sep close credible.

items.action: Send Kristopher the ROI data-collection form to build the low-volume savings case.
items.trigger: Zycus committed the ROI business case and buyer's decision is ROI-gated
items.expected_effect: Produces the quantified proof that defeats the 'too small for ROI' objection and unlocks budget authority.

items.action: Line up 2-3 comparable healthcare-MSO references for Kristopher.
items.trigger: Rival vendors told NVISION its low volume makes ROI unlikely
items.expected_effect: Breaks the vendor-fit skepticism with social proof from similar low-volume healthcare buyers.

items.action: Ask Kristopher to broker the CEO/CFO economic-buyer intro once the ROI case lands.
items.trigger: Budget sits above Kristopher and the CEO is the named AI sponsor but has never engaged
items.expected_effect: Maps and engages the power figure who signs a ~$130k buy, making Best Case defensible.

items.action: Multi-thread to Paul Hernandez and an IT owner to scope the Stampley and NV integrations.
items.trigger: Integration dependencies sit outside Kristopher's control and IT is unmapped
items.expected_effect: De-risks adoption and surfaces the InfoSec/integration path before contracting.

customer_preference.evidence: Positive but not decisive: praised the single platform ('I like that it's one platform', 7 May) and asked deep Zycus-specific questions (substitute-product sourcing events, Stampley integration), but stayed exploratory and ROI-gated with no clear lean.

business_case.evidence: Qualitative pain only, no numbers: manual Capex sourcing takes weeks, painful invoice price lookups, unstructured contract storage, but no dollar baseline or savings target ('I'd have to see what kind of savings', 15 Jun).

contributions.evidence: scope narrowed vs prior — Kristopher wants to start small: 'I don't wanna, like, just jump all in and get all the areas all at once, which is why I'm trying to narrow it down' (15 Jun);  (buyer likely defensive on cost or phased implementation)
customer requested next meeting (from call evidence).
Next Step carries 7 dated milestone(s) — actively worked.

commentary.win_position: Win 36 is carried by momentum 69 vs stage-expected 52; preference strength +0.40 (weight 20). Offsetting it: scope narrowed vs prior — Kristopher wants to start small: 'I don't wanna, like, just jump all in and get all the areas all at once, which….
commentary.deal_momentum: Momentum 69 (moving forward): Next Step carries 7 dated milestone(s) — actively worked. Silence alone was not treated as backward motion.
commentary.customer_commitment: Commitment sits near the earned-from-zero floor: little observable customer investment surfaced. Commitment is earned, so a low value is honest, not a penalty.
commentary.deal_risk: Risk 0: no break-risk was observed in the swept evidence. Coverage is thin, so read this as 'nothing seen yet,' not 'nothing there.'
commentary.forecast_confidence: Forecast confidence 30 is anchored on the stage win (36), nudged by commitment 8 and momentum 69, and dragged by risk 0. Read is Early Read — a confidence flag (we know less), not a haircut on the score.
commentary.evidence_coverage: Early Read: 1 of 7 evidence dimensions seen (forward motion). It labels how much of the picture we have, never the primary scores.
